At this point, you're beginning to realize that no is actually what you want to seek first. Before a moment, let's go back to the word yes. In negotiation, there are actually three versions of the word. Counterfeit, confirmation, and commitment. The first kind, the counterfeit yes, is when you feel like saying no, but you feel that yes is an easier escape route. You can use this type of yes in a deceitful manner to keep the conversation going so that you can obtain more information to get some kind of an edge. Secondly, a confirmation yes is generally innocent and is a reflex to some black or white question. In other words, it's just a simple affirmation with no promise of action. The final type of yes is commitment yes. This type is the one you most often have wanted when negotiating. If you heard this word, you would say bingo. This has been your most coveted form of yes because you felt it was a true agreement that you hoped would lead to action. The problem is yes is nothing without how. And even a commitment yes doesn't lay out how in any detail. Since all three types of yes sound almost identical, you have to be careful and learn how to recognize which one is being used. Great negotiators know that their job isn't to put on a great performance, but to gently guide their counterpart to discover the goal as their own. The most common form of yes is the counterfeit. You may not even realize you've done it countless times. People all over the world are so used to being pursued for the commitment yes that the counterfeit yes is used to find out more to be polite across the globe. People have become masters of this form. Additionally, they're afraid that if they say no, the other person will become offended. Let me ask you this. Have you ever told a friend you would hang out with them only to back out later with some excuse you invented at the last second?
